title: Phase 0 Loads Prior Run Findings as Seed Data
description: When previous quality runs exist, Phase 0 must automatically load their findings as seed data for the current run. This phase is conditional and only applies to re-runs, not initial runs.
acceptance_criteria: If previous quality runs exist, load their findings as seed data. This is automatic and only applies to re-runs.
proposed_source_ref: Plan Overview section, Phase 0 paragraph

title: Phase 1 Begins With Documentation Intake Command
description: Phase 1 must start by running the v1.5.2 documentation intake using the command `python -m bin.reference_docs_ingest <target>` to walk the `reference_docs/` directory before any other exploration activity.
acceptance_criteria: Run the v1.5.2 documentation intake first (`python -m bin.reference_docs_ingest <target>` to walk `reference_docs/`)
proposed_source_ref: Plan Overview section, Phase 1 paragraph

title: reference_docs/cite/ Files Produce formal_docs_manifest.json Records
description: During Phase 1 documentation intake, files found under the `cite/` subdirectory within `reference_docs/` must produce records written to `quality/formal_docs_manifest.json`.
acceptance_criteria: `cite/` files produce `quality/formal_docs_manifest.json` records
proposed_source_ref: Plan Overview section, Phase 1 paragraph

title: reference_docs/ Top-Level Files Loaded as Tier 4 Context
description: During Phase 1 documentation intake, top-level files in `reference_docs/` (not under `cite/`) must be loaded as Tier 4 context via the `reference_docs_ingest.load_tier4_context(<target>)` function.
acceptance_criteria: top-level files are loaded as Tier 4 context via `reference_docs_ingest.load_tier4_context(<target>)`
proposed_source_ref: Plan Overview section, Phase 1 paragraph

title: Phase 1 Exploration Follows Three Ordered Stages
description: Phase 1 codebase exploration must proceed through three stages in order: open exploration driven by domain knowledge, domain-knowledge risk analysis, and selected structured exploration patterns. These stages must not be skipped or reordered.
acceptance_criteria: explore the codebase in three stages: open exploration driven by domain knowledge, domain-knowledge risk analysis, and selected structured exploration patterns
proposed_source_ref: Plan Overview section, Phase 1 paragraph

title: Phase 1 Writes All Findings to EXPLORATION.md
description: All findings from Phase 1 must be written to `quality/EXPLORATION.md`. This file serves as the foundation for Phase 2.
acceptance_criteria: Write all findings to `quality/EXPLORATION.md`. This file is the foundation — Phase 2 reads it as its primary input.
proposed_source_ref: Plan Overview section, Phase 1 paragraph

title: Phase 2 Reads EXPLORATION.md as Primary Input
description: Phase 2 must read `quality/EXPLORATION.md` as its primary input before producing any quality artifacts. It must not generate artifacts without first reading the exploration findings.
acceptance_criteria: Read EXPLORATION.md and produce the quality artifacts
proposed_source_ref: Plan Overview section, Phase 2 paragraph

title: Phase 2 Produces All Eight Quality Artifacts
description: Phase 2 must produce all eight quality artifacts: requirements, constitution, functional tests, code review protocol, integration tests, spec audit protocol, TDD protocol, and AGENTS.md. No artifact may be omitted.
acceptance_criteria: produce the quality artifacts: requirements, constitution, functional tests, code review protocol, integration tests, spec audit protocol, TDD protocol, AGENTS.md
proposed_source_ref: Plan Overview section, Phase 2 paragraph

title: Phase 3 Runs Three-Pass Code Review Against HEAD
description: Phase 3 must execute a three-pass code review against the current HEAD of the repository. The review must consist of exactly three passes.
acceptance_criteria: Run the three-pass code review against HEAD.
proposed_source_ref: Plan Overview section, Phase 3 paragraph

title: Phase 3 Writes Regression Tests for Every Confirmed Bug
description: For every bug confirmed during Phase 3 code review, a regression test must be written. No confirmed bug may be left without a corresponding regression test.
acceptance_criteria: Write regression tests for every confirmed bug.
proposed_source_ref: Plan Overview section, Phase 3 paragraph

title: Phase 3 Generates Patches for Confirmed Bugs
description: Phase 3 must generate patches for confirmed bugs found during code review.
acceptance_criteria: Generate patches.
proposed_source_ref: Plan Overview section, Phase 3 paragraph

title: Phase 4 Uses Three Independent AI Auditors
description: Phase 4 spec audit must use exactly three independent AI auditors to review the code against requirements. The auditors must be independent of each other.
acceptance_criteria: Three independent AI auditors review the code against requirements.
proposed_source_ref: Plan Overview section, Phase 4 paragraph

title: Phase 4 Triages Findings With Verification Probes
description: After the three-auditor review, Phase 4 must triage the findings using verification probes before proceeding to the semantic citation check.
acceptance_criteria: Triage with verification probes.
proposed_source_ref: Plan Overview section, Phase 4 paragraph

title: Phase 4 Semantic Citation Check Runs After Triage
description: The Council's Layer-2 semantic citation check must run after triage is complete, not concurrently or before triage.
acceptance_criteria: After triage, the same Council runs the v1.5.2 Layer-2 semantic citation check
proposed_source_ref: Plan Overview section, Phase 4 paragraph

title: Semantic Citation Check Uses One Prompt Per Reviewer
description: The Layer-2 semantic citation check must issue exactly one prompt per reviewer (one per Council member), structured to produce per-REQ verdicts.
acceptance_criteria: one prompt per reviewer, structured per-REQ verdicts for every Tier 1/2 citation
proposed_source_ref: Plan Overview section, Phase 4 paragraph

title: Semantic Citation Check Produces Verdicts for Every Tier 1/2 Citation
description: The semantic citation check must produce structured per-REQ verdicts covering every Tier 1 and Tier 2 citation. No Tier 1/2 citation may be omitted from the verdict output.
acceptance_criteria: structured per-REQ verdicts for every Tier 1/2 citation
proposed_source_ref: Plan Overview section, Phase 4 paragraph

title: Semantic Citation Check Output Written to citation_semantic_check.json
description: The output of the Layer-2 semantic citation check must be written to `quality/citation_semantic_check.json`.
acceptance_criteria: output to `quality/citation_semantic_check.json`
proposed_source_ref: Plan Overview section, Phase 4 paragraph

title: Phase 4 Writes Regression Tests for Net-New Findings
description: Any net-new findings discovered during Phase 4 spec audit (not already found in Phase 3) must have regression tests written for them.
acceptance_criteria: Write regression tests for net-new findings.
proposed_source_ref: Plan Overview section, Phase 4 paragraph

title: Phase 5 Tracks Every Bug as Regression-Tested or Explicitly Exempted
description: Phase 5 must ensure that every bug found in Phase 3 and Phase 4 is either regression-tested or explicitly exempted. No bug may be silently dropped.
acceptance_criteria: every bug from code review and spec audit is tracked, regression-tested or explicitly exempted
proposed_source_ref: Plan Overview section, Phase 5 paragraph

title: Phase 5 Runs TDD Red-Green Cycle
description: Phase 5 must run the TDD red-green cycle to verify that regression tests fail on unpatched code and pass after patches are applied.
acceptance_criteria: Run TDD red-green cycle.
proposed_source_ref: Plan Overview section, Phase 5 paragraph

title: Phase 5 Finalizes Completeness Report
description: Phase 5 must produce and finalize the completeness report as its closing action.
acceptance_criteria: Finalize the completeness report.
proposed_source_ref: Plan Overview section, Phase 5 paragraph

title: Phase 6 Runs Self-Check Benchmarks Against All Generated Artifacts
description: Phase 6 must run self-check benchmarks against every artifact generated by the previous phases, not a subset.
acceptance_criteria: Run self-check benchmarks against all generated artifacts.
proposed_source_ref: Plan Overview section, Phase 6 paragraph

title: Phase 6 Checks Internal Consistency of Artifacts
description: Phase 6 must verify internal consistency across the generated artifacts, checking for contradictions or misalignments between them.
acceptance_criteria: Check for internal consistency
proposed_source_ref: Plan Overview section, Phase 6 paragraph

title: Phase 6 Checks Version Stamp Correctness
description: Phase 6 must verify that version stamps in all generated artifacts are correct and match the expected skill version.
acceptance_criteria: version stamp correctness
proposed_source_ref: Plan Overview section, Phase 6 paragraph

title: Phase 6 Checks Convergence
description: Phase 6 must check for convergence across the generated artifacts, verifying that the quality system has stabilized.
acceptance_criteria: and convergence.
proposed_source_ref: Plan Overview section, Phase 6 paragraph

title: Phase 7 Presents Results With Scannable Summary Table
description: Phase 7 must present results to the user using a scannable summary table format, not a prose-only presentation.
acceptance_criteria: Present results to the user with a scannable summary table
proposed_source_ref: Plan Overview section, Phase 7 paragraph

title: Phase 7 Offers Drill-Down on Any Artifact
description: Phase 7 must offer the user the ability to drill down into any individual artifact from the quality run.
acceptance_criteria: offer drill-down on any artifact
proposed_source_ref: Plan Overview section, Phase 7 paragraph

title: Phase 7 Provides Menu of Improvement Paths
description: Phase 7 must provide the user with a menu of improvement paths including at minimum: iteration strategies, requirement refinement, and integration test tuning.
acceptance_criteria: provide a menu of improvement paths (iteration strategies, requirement refinement, integration test tuning)
proposed_source_ref: Plan Overview section, Phase 7 paragraph

title: MANDATORY FIRST ACTION Prints Specified Banner Message
description: Before any other output, the skill must print the exact specified banner message: 'Quality Playbook v1.5.2 — by Andrew Stellman' followed by the GitHub URL. This is labeled MANDATORY and must precede the plan explanation.
acceptance_criteria:
print the following message to the user: > Quality Playbook v1.5.2 — by Andrew Stellman
> https://github.com/andrewstellman/quality-playbook
proposed_source_ref: Plan Overview section, MANDATORY FIRST ACTION paragraph

title: Plan Explanation Must Be in Own Words, Not Verbatim Copy
description: After printing the banner, the skill must explain the plan in its own words to demonstrate understanding. It must not copy the plan verbatim.
acceptance_criteria: Do not copy the plan verbatim; paraphrase it to demonstrate understanding.
proposed_source_ref: Plan Overview section, MANDATORY FIRST ACTION paragraph

title: Plan Explanation Covers Each Phase's Output and Exploration's Primacy
description: The mandatory plan explanation must cover what each phase produces and must emphasize why the exploration phase matters most.
acceptance_criteria: explain the plan in your own words — what you'll do, what each phase produces, and why the exploration phase matters most
proposed_source_ref: Plan Overview section, MANDATORY FIRST ACTION paragraph

title: Plan Explanation Describes Three-Stage Exploration Structure
description: The plan explanation must specifically describe that exploration starts with open-ended domain-driven investigation, followed by domain-knowledge risk analysis, then supplemented by selected structured patterns. All three stages must be named.
acceptance_criteria: Emphasize that exploration starts with open-ended domain-driven investigation, followed by domain-knowledge risk analysis that reasons about what goes wrong in systems like this, then supplemented by selected structured patterns.
proposed_source_ref: Plan Overview section, MANDATORY FIRST ACTION paragraph

title: Run each phase in its own isolated session
description: The playbook is designed to run one phase at a time, with each phase executing in its own session with a clean context window. Phases produce files on disk that the subsequent phase reads, ensuring no cross-contamination of context between phases.
acceptance_criteria: Each phase runs in its own session with a clean context window, producing files on disk that the next phase reads.
proposed_source_ref: SKILL.md §How to Use, lines 210–215

title: Phase isolation yields better results than combined execution
description: Running phases one at a time produces better results than running all phases in a single session because each phase gets the full context window for deep analysis instead of competing for space with other phases.
acceptance_criteria: Each phase gets the full context window for deep analysis instead of competing for space with other phases.
proposed_source_ref: SKILL.md §How to Use, lines 210–215

title: Default invocation runs Phase 1 only and stops
description: When a user says 'run the quality playbook' or 'execute the quality playbook', the default behavior is to run Phase 1 (Explore) and stop. The skill must not automatically continue to Phase 2 or beyond without explicit user direction.
acceptance_criteria: When someone says 'run the quality playbook' or 'execute the quality playbook,' run Phase 1 (Explore) and stop.
proposed_source_ref: SKILL.md §How to Use, lines 210–215

title: Report phase outcome and continuation prompt after Phase 1
description: After Phase 1 completes, the skill must tell the user what happened and provide the exact prompt the user should say to continue to the next phase. This bridges the per-session model by explicitly handing control back to the user.
acceptance_criteria: After Phase 1 completes, tell the user what happened and what to say next.
proposed_source_ref: SKILL.md §How to Use, lines 210–215

title: User explicitly drives each phase transition
description: Phase progression is user-driven, not automatic. The skill waits for an explicit user instruction before starting any subsequent phase, ensuring each phase runs in its own session rather than chaining automatically.
acceptance_criteria: The user drives each phase forward explicitly.
proposed_source_ref: SKILL.md §How to Use, lines 210–215

title: Phase 0 runs only when prior quality artifacts exist
description: Phase 0 is conditional: it executes only if the directory quality/runs/ exists AND contains prior quality artifacts (subdirectories with quality/BUGS.md under them). If quality/runs/ does not exist, the playbook skips Phase 0 entirely and proceeds directly to Phase 1.
acceptance_criteria: If there are no prior runs, skip to Phase 1.
proposed_source_ref: SKILL.md §Phase 0: Prior Run Analysis (Automatic), opening paragraph

title: Skip Phase 0a and fall through to Phase 0b when quality/runs/ is empty or non-conformant
description: If quality/runs/ exists but is empty or contains no conformant quality artifacts (no subdirectories with quality/BUGS.md under them), the playbook must skip Step 0a specifically and fall through to Step 0b rather than skipping Phase 0 entirely.
acceptance_criteria: If quality/runs/ exists but is empty or contains no conformant quality artifacts (no subdirectories with quality/BUGS.md under them), skip Phase 0a and fall through to Phase 0b.
proposed_source_ref: SKILL.md §Phase 0: Prior Run Analysis (Automatic), opening paragraph

title: Prior runs enable continuation mode for iterative bug discovery
description: When prior runs exist, the playbook enters continuation mode. In continuation mode each run inherits confirmed findings from prior runs, verifies them mechanically, and explores for additional bugs. The iteration is designed to converge when a run finds zero net-new bugs.
acceptance_criteria: When prior runs exist, the playbook enters continuation mode.
proposed_source_ref: SKILL.md §Phase 0: Prior Run Analysis (Automatic), continuation mode paragraph

title: Step 0a reads BUGS.md from all prior runs
description: Step 0a requires reading quality/runs/*/quality/BUGS.md from all prior runs, not just the most recent one. Every prior run's confirmed bugs must be included in the seed list construction.
acceptance_criteria: Read quality/runs/*/quality/BUGS.md from all prior runs.
proposed_source_ref: SKILL.md §Phase 0 Step 0a: Build the seed list

title: Step 0a extracts four fields per confirmed bug
description: For each confirmed bug found in prior BUGS.md files, Step 0a must extract exactly four fields: bug ID, file:line, summary, and the regression test assertion. All four fields are required for each entry in the seed list.
acceptance_criteria: For each confirmed bug, extract: bug ID, file:line, summary, and the regression test assertion.
proposed_source_ref: SKILL.md §Phase 0 Step 0a: Build the seed list

title: Step 0a deduplicates seeds by file:line
description: When the same bug appears in multiple prior runs, it must be counted only once in the merged seed list. The deduplication key is the file:line field; bugs with identical file:line locations from different runs are collapsed into a single seed entry.
acceptance_criteria: Deduplicate by file:line (the same bug found in multiple runs counts once).
proposed_source_ref: SKILL.md §Phase 0 Step 0a: Build the seed list

title: Step 0a writes merged seed list to quality/SEED_CHECKS.md in required format
description: After reading and deduplicating, Step 0a must write the merged seed list to quality/SEED_CHECKS.md using the exact prescribed markdown table format: a heading indicating the count of prior runs, followed by a table with columns Seed, Origin Run, File:Line, Summary, and Assertion.
acceptance_criteria: Write the merged seed list to quality/SEED_CHECKS.md with the specified markdown table format.
proposed_source_ref: SKILL.md §Phase 0 Step 0a: Build the seed list, code block

title: Step 0b runs each seed assertion against the current source tree
description: For each seed produced by Step 0a, Step 0b must mechanically execute the seed's regression test assertion against the current source tree. The word 'mechanically' signals that this is not a subjective re-review but an objective assertion check.
acceptance_criteria: For each seed, run the assertion against the current source tree.
proposed_source_ref: SKILL.md §Phase 0 Step 0b: Execute seed checks mechanically

title: Step 0b records PASS or FAIL for each seed
description: Each seed check must be recorded as either PASS (the bug was fixed since the last run) or FAIL (the bug is still present). These are the only two valid outcomes; ambiguous results must resolve to one of these states.
acceptance_criteria: Record PASS (bug was fixed since last run) or FAIL (bug still present).
proposed_source_ref: SKILL.md §Phase 0 Step 0b: Execute seed checks mechanically

title: Failing seeds must appear in this run's BUGS.md unconditionally
description: A seed that fails its assertion check is a confirmed carry-forward bug and must be included in the current run's BUGS.md regardless of whether any auditor independently discovers it during Phases 3–5. Auditor non-discovery does not override mechanical confirmation.
acceptance_criteria: A failing seed is a confirmed carry-forward bug — it must appear in this run's BUGS.md regardless of whether any auditor independently finds it.
proposed_source_ref: SKILL.md §Phase 0 Step 0b: Execute seed checks mechanically

title: Passing seeds are noted in PROGRESS.md as resolved
description: A seed that passes its assertion check (bug no longer present) must be recorded in PROGRESS.md with the notation 'SEED-NNN: resolved since prior run', using the seed's identifier. These entries confirm the bug was fixed and prevent future runs from re-injecting it.
acceptance_criteria: A passing seed means the bug was fixed — note it in PROGRESS.md as 'SEED-NNN: resolved since prior run'.
proposed_source_ref: SKILL.md §Phase 0 Step 0b: Execute seed checks mechanically

title: Step 0c reads prior PROGRESS.md files to identify covered subsystems
description: Step 0c requires reading quality/runs/*/quality/PROGRESS.md from all prior runs and extracting scope declarations that indicate which subsystems were covered. This information is used to guide Phase 1 exploration toward uncovered territory.
acceptance_criteria: Read quality/runs/*/quality/PROGRESS.md for scope declarations. Note which subsystems were covered in prior runs.
proposed_source_ref: SKILL.md §Phase 0 Step 0c: Identify prior-run scope

title: Step 0c directs Phase 1 to prioritize uncovered subsystems
description: During Phase 1 exploration, the playbook must prioritize areas that were NOT covered by prior runs. This prioritization is derived from the scope declarations extracted in Step 0c and is intended to maximize the chance of finding new bugs.
acceptance_criteria: During Phase 1 exploration, prioritize areas NOT covered by prior runs to maximize the chance of finding new bugs.
proposed_source_ref: SKILL.md §Phase 0 Step 0c: Identify prior-run scope

title: When all subsystems were previously covered, explore with different emphasis
description: If Step 0c determines that all subsystems were already covered in prior runs, Phase 1 must still explore the same scope but with different emphasis — for example different scrutiny areas or different entry points — rather than repeating the identical exploration.
acceptance_criteria: If all subsystems were covered in prior runs, explore the same scope but with different emphasis (e.g., different scrutiny areas, different entry points).
proposed_source_ref: SKILL.md §Phase 0 Step 0c: Identify prior-run scope

title: Step 0d injects seeds into Phase 3 code review prompt
description: The seed list must be injected into the Phase 3 code review prompt with the specified language: 'Prior runs confirmed these bugs — verify they are still present and look for additional findings in the same subsystems.' This ensures code reviewers focus attention on known-vulnerable subsystems.
acceptance_criteria: Phase 3 (code review): Add to the code review prompt: 'Prior runs confirmed these bugs — verify they are still present and look for additional findings in the same subsystems.'
proposed_source_ref: SKILL.md §Phase 0 Step 0d: Inject seeds into downstream phases

title: Step 0d injects seeds into Phase 4 RUN_SPEC_AUDIT.md with coverage-gap framing
description: The seed list must be injected into Phase 4's RUN_SPEC_AUDIT.md with the specified framing: 'Known open issues from prior runs: [seed list]. Expect auditors to find these. If an auditor does NOT flag a known seed bug, that is a coverage gap in their review, not evidence the bug was fixed.' This prevents false-negative auditor results from being misconstrued as confirmations that bugs are fixed.
acceptance_criteria: Phase 4 (spec audit): Add to RUN_SPEC_AUDIT.md: 'Known open issues from prior runs: [seed list]. Expect auditors to find these. If an auditor does NOT flag a known seed bug, that is a coverage gap in their review, not evidence the bug was fixed.'
proposed_source_ref: SKILL.md §Phase 0 Step 0d: Inject seeds into downstream phases

title: Ask Chat-History Question Before Code Exploration
description: Before exploring any code, the skill must ask the user exactly one question about whether they have exported AI chat history from developing the project. This question must be posed as the first action of Step 0, prior to any file reading or code analysis.
acceptance_criteria: Before exploring code, ask the user one question about exported AI chat history.
proposed_source_ref: Step 0 opening paragraph, line ~449–451

title: Chat-History Question Covers Required Source Types
description: The single question asked must enumerate the specific types of chat history that are relevant: Claude exports, Gemini takeouts, ChatGPT exports, Claude Code transcripts, or similar. The question must also communicate the benefit to the user (better quality playbook).
acceptance_criteria: The question asks about: Claude exports, Gemini takeouts, ChatGPT exports, Claude Code transcripts, or similar.
proposed_source_ref: Step 0, quoted question block, line ~451–453

title: Scan for Index File First When Chat History Provided
description: When the user provides a chat history folder, the skill's first action must be to look for an index or navigation file before reading any chat content. The skill must look for files named INDEX*, CONTEXT.md, README.md, or similar navigation aids.
acceptance_criteria: Look for files named INDEX*, CONTEXT.md, README.md, or similar navigation aids. If one exists, read it.
proposed_source_ref: Step 0, numbered list item 1, line ~455–457

title: Read Index File If Found
description: If a navigation or index file is found in the chat history folder, the skill must read it before searching the rest of the folder. The rationale is that the index communicates what is present and how to find things.
acceptance_criteria: If an index file exists, read it — it will tell you what's there and how to find things.
proposed_source_ref: Step 0, numbered list item 1, line ~456–457

title: Search Chat History for Quality-Relevant Keywords
description: When searching the chat history, the skill must specifically search for messages mentioning the following keywords: quality, testing, coverage, bugs, failures, incidents, crashes, validation, retry, recovery, spec, fitness, audit, review.
acceptance_criteria: Look for messages mentioning: quality, testing, coverage, bugs, failures, incidents, crashes, validation, retry, recovery, spec, fitness, audit, review.
proposed_source_ref: Step 0, numbered list item 2, line ~458–459

title: Search Chat History for Project Name
description: In addition to quality-relevant keywords, the skill must also search for the project name itself when scanning chat history for relevant conversations.
acceptance_criteria: Also search for the project name.
proposed_source_ref: Step 0, numbered list item 2, line ~459

title: Extract Incident Reports from Chat History
description: When extracting content from chat history, the skill must specifically identify and extract incident reports, capturing what went wrong, how many records were affected, and how the incident was detected.
acceptance_criteria: Extract incident reports — what went wrong, how many records affected, how it was detected.
proposed_source_ref: Step 0, numbered list item 3, sub-item (a), line ~461

title: Extract Design Discussions from Chat History
description: The skill must extract design discussions from chat history, focusing on why a particular approach was chosen and what alternatives were rejected.
acceptance_criteria: Extract design discussions — why a particular approach was chosen, what alternatives were rejected.
proposed_source_ref: Step 0, numbered list item 3, sub-item (b), line ~462

title: Extract Quality Framework Discussions from Chat History
description: The skill must extract quality framework discussions from chat history, covering coverage targets, testing philosophy, and model review experiences.
acceptance_criteria: Extract quality framework discussions — coverage targets, testing philosophy, model review experiences.
proposed_source_ref: Step 0, numbered list item 3, sub-item (c), line ~463

title: Extract Cross-Model Feedback from Chat History
description: The skill must extract instances of cross-model feedback from chat history — specifically identifying conversations where different AI models disagreed about the code.
acceptance_criteria: Extract cross-model feedback — where different AI models disagreed about the code.
proposed_source_ref: Step 0, numbered list item 3, sub-item (d), line ~464

title: Use Targeted Index-Based Search Rather Than Exhaustive Reading
description: The skill must use the index to navigate to the most relevant conversations and search within those, rather than attempting to read all chat history exhaustively. The constraint is explicitly stated: use targeted search, not exhaustive reading.
acceptance_criteria: Don't try to read everything. Use the index to find the most relevant conversations, then search within those for quality-related content.
proposed_source_ref: Step 0, numbered list item 4, line ~465–466

title: Skip Step 0 Question in Autonomous/Benchmark Mode
description: When running in benchmark mode, via bin/run_playbook.py, or without user interaction (e.g., --single-pass), the skill must skip Step 0's question and proceed directly to Step 1 without waiting for user input.
acceptance_criteria: When running in benchmark mode, via bin/run_playbook.py, or without user interaction (e.g., --single-pass), skip Step 0's question and proceed directly to Step 1.
proposed_source_ref: Step 0, Autonomous fallback paragraph, line ~469

title: Scan Visible Chat History Folders Without Asking in Autonomous Mode
description: In autonomous mode, if chat history folders are visible in the project tree (such as AI Chat History/ or .chat_exports/), the skill must scan them without asking the user for permission or confirmation.
acceptance_criteria: If chat history folders are visible in the project tree (e.g., AI Chat History/, .chat_exports/), scan them without asking.
proposed_source_ref: Step 0, Autonomous fallback paragraph, line ~470

title: Do Not Block Waiting for User Response in Autonomous Mode
description: In autonomous mode, if no chat history is found, the skill must proceed to the next step without blocking or waiting for a user response. The skill must not halt execution waiting for input that will never arrive.
acceptance_criteria: If no chat history is found, proceed — do not block waiting for a response that won't come.
proposed_source_ref: Step 0, Autonomous fallback paragraph, line ~471

title: Proceed Normally When User Has No Chat History
description: If the user indicates they do not have chat history, the skill must continue with the remaining steps without degradation or blocking. The skill is designed to function without chat history context.
acceptance_criteria: If the user doesn't have chat history, proceed normally — the skill works without it, just with less context.
proposed_source_ref: Step 0, final paragraph before Autonomous fallback, line ~468

title: Read Project Documentation and Build Config
description: At the start of Step 1, the skill must read the README, existing documentation, and build config files before answering any project-characterization questions. Applicable build configs include pyproject.toml, package.json, and Cargo.toml.
acceptance_criteria: The skill reads README, existing documentation, and build config (pyproject.toml / package.json / Cargo.toml) before producing project characterization answers.
proposed_source_ref: Step 1 opening paragraph, line 469

title: Produce One-Sentence Project Description
description: Step 1 requires the skill to answer 'What does this project do?' in exactly one sentence. This single-sentence summary must appear in the skill's output before proceeding to further exploration.
acceptance_criteria: The skill produces a one-sentence answer to 'What does this project do?'
proposed_source_ref: Step 1 bullet list, line 471: 'What does this project do? (One sentence.)'

title: Identify Language and Key Dependencies
description: Step 1 requires the skill to answer 'What language and key dependencies?' This answer must be present in the skill's output as part of the project characterization.
acceptance_criteria: The skill identifies the project's language and key dependencies.
proposed_source_ref: Step 1 bullet list, line 472: 'What language and key dependencies?'

title: Identify External Systems
description: Step 1 requires the skill to answer 'What external systems does it talk to?' This answer must appear in the project characterization output.
acceptance_criteria: The skill identifies all external systems the project communicates with.
proposed_source_ref: Step 1 bullet list, line 473: 'What external systems does it talk to?'

title: Identify Primary Output
description: Step 1 requires the skill to answer 'What is the primary output?' This must be captured in the project characterization output.
acceptance_criteria: The skill identifies and records the primary output of the project.
proposed_source_ref: Step 1 bullet list, line 474: 'What is the primary output?'

title: Search for Specifications in Defined Order
description: The skill must search for specification documents in a specific, fixed order: AGENTS.md/CLAUDE.md in root, then specs/, docs/, spec/, design/, architecture/, adr/, then .md files in root. This order must not be varied or shortcut.
acceptance_criteria: Specs are searched in order: AGENTS.md/CLAUDE.md in root, specs/, docs/, spec/, design/, architecture/, adr/, then .md files in root.
proposed_source_ref: Step 1 'Find the specifications' paragraph, line 476: 'Search in order: AGENTS.md/CLAUDE.md in root, specs/, docs/, spec/, design/, architecture/, adr/, then .md files in root.'

title: Record Specification File Paths
description: After locating specification documents, the skill must record the file paths of all found specs. These paths serve as the authoritative source-of-truth references for subsequent passes.
acceptance_criteria: The skill records the paths of all found specification documents.
proposed_source_ref: Step 1 'Find the specifications' paragraph, line 476: 'Record the paths.'

title: Operate Without Formal Spec Documents
description: If no formal spec documents are found, the skill must still proceed and produce requirements. The absence of formal specs is not a blocking condition; the skill must fall back to alternative sources.
acceptance_criteria: If no formal spec documents exist, the skill still works and continues to assemble requirements from alternative sources.
proposed_source_ref: Step 1 'If no formal spec documents exist' paragraph, line 478: 'the skill still works — but you need to assemble requirements from other sources'

title: Use Alternative Requirement Sources in Defined Priority Order
description: When no formal spec exists, the skill must use alternative sources in a specific priority order: (1) ask the user, (2) README and inline documentation, (3) existing test suite, (4) type signatures and validation rules, (5) infer from code behavior. This order must be respected.
acceptance_criteria: When working from non-formal requirements, alternative sources are used in order: (1) Ask the user, (2) README and inline documentation, (3) Existing test suite, (4) Type signatures and validation rules, (5) Infer from code behavior.
proposed_source_ref: Step 1 numbered list, lines 480–484: 'In order of preference: 1. Ask the user … 5. Infer from code behavior'

title: Label Inferred Requirements in QUALITY.md
description: Requirements deduced solely from reading code behavior (the last-resort fallback) must be explicitly labeled as inferred requirements in QUALITY.md so reviewers can distinguish them from authoritative requirements.
acceptance_criteria: Inferred requirements are marked as *inferred requirements* in QUALITY.md.
proposed_source_ref: Step 1 item 5, line 484: 'Mark these as inferred requirements in QUALITY.md'

title: Flag Inferred Requirements for User Confirmation
description: Any requirement inferred from code behavior must be flagged for user confirmation. Inferred requirements must not be treated as authoritative without this confirmation step.
acceptance_criteria: Inferred requirements are flagged for user confirmation.
proposed_source_ref: Step 1 item 5, line 484: 'flag them for user confirmation'

title: Attach Requirement Tag Including Confidence Tier and Source
description: When working from non-formal requirements, each scenario and test must be labeled with a requirement tag that encodes both a confidence tier (formal, user-confirmed, or inferred) and the specific source of the requirement.
acceptance_criteria: Each scenario and test is labeled with a requirement tag that includes a confidence tier and source, e.g., '[Req: formal — README §3]', '[Req: user-confirmed — "must handle empty input"]', '[Req: inferred — from validate_input() behavior]'.
proposed_source_ref: Step 1 'When working from non-formal requirements' paragraph, lines 486–490

title: Use Exact Requirement Tag Format
description: The requirement tag format must match exactly: [Req: <tier> — <source>]. The three defined tiers are 'formal', 'user-confirmed', and 'inferred'. The dash must be an em-dash (—). No variation in format is permitted.
acceptance_criteria: Requirement tags use the exact format '[Req: <tier> — <source>]' with tiers limited to 'formal', 'user-confirmed', and 'inferred'.
proposed_source_ref: Step 1 tag format bullets, lines 488–490: 'Use this exact tag format'

title: Apply Requirement Tags in QUALITY.md Scenarios, Functional Tests, and Spec Audit Findings
description: The requirement tag format must be applied consistently across three specific artifact types: QUALITY.md scenarios, functional test documentation, and spec audit findings. Tags must not be omitted from any of these three locations.
acceptance_criteria: Requirement tags appear in QUALITY.md scenarios, functional test documentation, and spec audit findings.
proposed_source_ref: Step 1 final sentence, line 491: 'Use this exact tag format in QUALITY.md scenarios, functional test documentation, and spec audit findings.'

title: Read All docs_gathered/ Files Before Scoping
description: Before deciding which subsystems to focus on, the skill must read every file in docs_gathered/ if that directory exists. Scoping decisions must not be made until all available documentation has been reviewed.
acceptance_criteria: If `docs_gathered/` exists, read every file in it before deciding which subsystems to focus on.
proposed_source_ref: Step 1b section, opening paragraph

title: Classify Each Document's Depth as Deep, Moderate, or Shallow
description: For each document in docs_gathered/, the skill must classify its documentation depth using the defined taxonomy: Deep (internal contracts, safety invariants, concurrency models, defensive patterns, error handling details, or line-number-level source references), Moderate (architecture and API surface with some implementation detail), or Shallow (API catalog, feature overview, or marketing-level summary).
acceptance_criteria: For each document, classify its depth as Deep, Moderate, or Shallow per the definitions provided.
proposed_source_ref: Step 1b section, depth classification bullet list

title: Do Not Narrow Audit Scope to Only Well-Documented Subsystems
description: The skill must not restrict its audit scope to subsystems that have deep documentation. High-risk or failure-prone modules with only shallow documentation must still be included in the audit scope. Limiting scope to well-documented areas is explicitly prohibited.
acceptance_criteria: Do not narrow the audit scope to only the subsystems that have deep documentation.
proposed_source_ref: Step 1b section, 'The scoping rule' paragraph

title: Flag Documentation Gaps for High-Risk Shallow-Documented Modules in PROGRESS.md
description: When the most complex or most failure-prone module has only shallow documentation, the skill must treat this as a documentation gap to flag in PROGRESS.md, not as a reason to skip auditing the module.
acceptance_criteria: If the most complex or most failure-prone module has only shallow documentation, that is a documentation gap to flag in PROGRESS.md, not a reason to skip the module.
proposed_source_ref: Step 1b section, 'The scoping rule' paragraph

title: Record Documentation Gap Under Named Section in PROGRESS.md
description: When documentation is shallow for a high-risk area, the skill must note the gap explicitly in PROGRESS.md under a section heading named exactly '## Documentation depth assessment'.
acceptance_criteria: Note the gap explicitly in PROGRESS.md under a `## Documentation depth assessment` section.
proposed_source_ref: Step 1b section, 'When documentation is shallow' list item 1

title: Derive Requirements From Source Code for Shallow-Documented High-Risk Areas
description: When documentation for a high-risk area is shallow, the skill must derive requirements directly from the source code — including doc comments, safety annotations, defensive patterns, and existing tests — rather than skipping requirement derivation.
acceptance_criteria: Derive requirements from source code directly (doc comments, safety annotations, defensive patterns, existing tests) and tag them as `[Req: inferred — from source]`.
proposed_source_ref: Step 1b section, 'When documentation is shallow' list item 2

title: Tag Source-Inferred Requirements With Correct Tag Format
description: Requirements derived from source code for shallow-documented high-risk areas must be tagged with the exact string `[Req: inferred — from source]` to distinguish them from formally or user-confirmed requirements.
acceptance_criteria: Tag them as `[Req: inferred — from source]`.
proposed_source_ref: Step 1b section, 'When documentation is shallow' list item 2

title: Flag Shallow High-Risk Areas for Deeper Documentation in Completeness Report
description: When a high-risk area has only shallow documentation, the skill must flag the area for deeper documentation gathering in the completeness report, in addition to noting the gap in PROGRESS.md.
acceptance_criteria: Flag the area for deeper documentation gathering in the completeness report.
proposed_source_ref: Step 1b section, 'When documentation is shallow' list item 3

title: Record Depth Classification for Each docs_gathered/ File in PROGRESS.md
description: The skill must record the depth classification (Deep, Moderate, or Shallow) for each file in docs_gathered/ in PROGRESS.md so reviewers can assess whether the documentation influenced the audit scope appropriately.
acceptance_criteria: Record the depth classification for each `docs_gathered/` file in PROGRESS.md so reviewers can assess whether the documentation influenced the scope appropriately.
proposed_source_ref: Step 1b section, paragraph following 'When documentation is shallow' list

title: Produce Coverage Commitment Table in PROGRESS.md
description: After classifying all docs_gathered/ documents, the skill must produce a coverage commitment table in PROGRESS.md under the '## Documentation depth assessment' section. The table must be present and populated before proceeding.
acceptance_criteria: After classifying all `docs_gathered/` documents, produce this table in PROGRESS.md under the `## Documentation depth assessment` section.
proposed_source_ref: Step 1b section, 'Coverage commitment table' paragraph

title: Coverage Commitment Table Must Include All Required Columns
description: The coverage commitment table produced in PROGRESS.md must contain exactly the five columns specified: Document, Depth, Subsystem, Requirements commitment, and 'If excluded: justification'.
acceptance_criteria: Table columns: Document | Depth | Subsystem | Requirements commitment | If excluded: justification
proposed_source_ref: Step 1b section, table definition under 'Coverage commitment table'

title: Map Every Deep Document to Its Subsystem in the Commitment Table
description: For every document classified as Deep, the skill must map it to the subsystem it covers in the coverage commitment table, and either commit to deriving requirements from it or provide a specific justification for exclusion.
acceptance_criteria: For every deep document, map it to the subsystem it covers, then either commit to deriving requirements from it ('will cover in Phase 2') or provide a specific justification.
proposed_source_ref: Step 1b section, paragraph following table definition

title: Exclusion Justification for Deep Documents Must Be Specific
description: A justification of 'out of scope for this run' is not sufficient for excluding a deep document from the requirements set. The justification must name the specific tradeoff — for example, explaining which pipeline is in scope and recommending a separate run for the excluded subsystem.
acceptance_criteria: A sentence like 'out of scope for this run' is not sufficient — the justification must say why, e.g., 'interpreter JIT is excluded because this run focuses on the parser/compiler/GC pipeline; separate run recommended.'
proposed_source_ref: Step 1b section, paragraph following table definition

title: Gate: High-Risk Deeply-Documented Subsystem Must Not Disappear From Requirements
description: A high-risk subsystem that is documented deeply in docs_gathered/ must not silently produce zero requirements in the final requirements set. The gate enforces that deep documentation coverage translates into actual requirement derivation.
acceptance_criteria: A high-risk subsystem documented deeply in `docs_gathered/` must not silently disappear from the requirements set.
proposed_source_ref: Step 1b section, 'Gate' paragraph

title: Gate: Unmet 'Will Cover' Commitment Blocks Proceeding to Phase 2
description: If a deep document has a 'will cover in Phase 2' commitment in the coverage commitment table but produces zero requirements by the end of Step 7, the skill must go back and derive requirements for that gap before proceeding to Phase 2 artifact generation. A zero-requirement outcome for a committed deep document is a pipeline incompleteness, not a valid result.
acceptance_criteria: If a deep document has a 'will cover' commitment but produces zero requirements by the end of Step 7, the requirements pipeline is incomplete — go back and derive requirements for the gap before proceeding to Phase 2 artifact generation.
proposed_source_ref: Step 1b section, 'Gate' paragraph

title: List Source Directories and Their Purposes
description: During Step 2 (Map the Architecture), the agent must enumerate all source directories in the project and record a description of each directory's purpose. This is required before identifying subsystems and is the first action listed in the step.
acceptance_criteria: The architecture mapping output lists source directories and their purposes.
proposed_source_ref: Step 2: Map the Architecture, bullet preamble, line 524

title: Read the Main Entry Point
description: During Step 2, the agent must locate and read the project's main entry point file. This is an explicit required action in the step before any subsystem identification occurs.
acceptance_criteria: The agent reads the main entry point as part of architecture mapping.
proposed_source_ref: Step 2: Map the Architecture, bullet preamble, line 524

title: Trace Execution Flow from Entry Point
description: During Step 2, after reading the main entry point, the agent must trace the execution flow of the system. This produces the understanding of control flow needed to identify subsystems and data flow.
acceptance_criteria: Execution flow is traced as part of the architecture mapping step.
proposed_source_ref: Step 2: Map the Architecture, bullet preamble, line 524

title: Identify 3–5 Major Subsystems
description: Step 2 requires the agent to identify between three and five major subsystems within the project. The numeric bounds (3–5) are explicit; the agent must neither under-identify (fewer than 3) nor over-fragment (more than 5) the architecture.
acceptance_criteria: The architecture map identifies the 3–5 major subsystems.
proposed_source_ref: Step 2: Map the Architecture, first bullet, line 526

title: Map the Data Flow as Input → Processing → Output
description: Step 2 requires the agent to identify and record the data flow of the system in the explicit three-stage model: Input → Processing → Output. This structured decomposition is a required deliverable of the architecture mapping step.
acceptance_criteria: The architecture map records the data flow (Input → Processing → Output).
proposed_source_ref: Step 2: Map the Architecture, second bullet, line 527

title: Identify the Most Complex Module
description: Step 2 requires the agent to identify which module is the most complex within the project. This identification must be explicit and singular — one module named as most complex — as it informs where testing effort should be concentrated.
acceptance_criteria: The architecture map identifies the most complex module.
proposed_source_ref: Step 2: Map the Architecture, third bullet, line 528

title: Identify the Most Fragile Module
description: Step 2 requires the agent to identify which module is the most fragile within the project. Fragility is distinct from complexity; the agent must assess and name the module most likely to break under unexpected inputs, changes, or edge conditions.
acceptance_criteria: The architecture map identifies the most fragile module.
proposed_source_ref: Step 2: Map the Architecture, fourth bullet, line 529

title: Read All Test Files for Small/Medium Projects or Representative Sample for Large
description: When executing Step 3, the agent must read all existing test files for small and medium projects. For large projects, the agent must read a representative sample from each subsystem rather than selecting arbitrarily from a single area.
acceptance_criteria: Read the existing test files — all of them for small/medium projects, or a representative sample from each subsystem for large ones.
proposed_source_ref: Step 3: Read Existing Tests, paragraph 1

title: Identify Test Count from Existing Tests
description: As part of Step 3, the agent must identify and record the total count of existing tests in the project. This count is part of the structured analysis output from reading the test files.
acceptance_criteria: Identify: test count, coverage patterns, gaps, and any coverage theater
proposed_source_ref: Step 3: Read Existing Tests, paragraph 1

title: Identify Coverage Patterns from Existing Tests
description: As part of Step 3, the agent must identify and record coverage patterns observed across the existing test suite, describing how the tests are distributed across the codebase and what types of assertions are most common.
acceptance_criteria: Identify: test count, coverage patterns, gaps, and any coverage theater
proposed_source_ref: Step 3: Read Existing Tests, paragraph 1

title: Identify Coverage Gaps from Existing Tests
description: As part of Step 3, the agent must identify gaps in the existing test coverage — areas of the codebase that have no or insufficient test coverage — and record these gaps for use in subsequent steps.
acceptance_criteria: Identify: test count, coverage patterns, gaps, and any coverage theater
proposed_source_ref: Step 3: Read Existing Tests, paragraph 1

title: Identify Coverage Theater in Existing Tests
description: As part of Step 3, the agent must identify coverage theater — tests that appear to provide meaningful coverage but do not actually catch real bugs. These tests must be flagged explicitly as part of the Step 3 analysis.
acceptance_criteria: any coverage theater (tests that look good but don't catch real bugs)
proposed_source_ref: Step 3: Read Existing Tests, paragraph 1

title: Record the Import Pattern Used by Existing Tests
description: The agent must record the exact import pattern used by existing tests to import project modules. This is marked Critical in the specification. The recorded pattern must cover the language-specific mechanism (e.g., Python sys.path manipulation, Java/Scala package imports, TypeScript relative paths or aliases, Go package/module paths, Rust use crate:: or use myproject::).
acceptance_criteria: Critical: Record the import pattern. How do existing tests import project modules?
proposed_source_ref: Step 3: Read Existing Tests, paragraph 2 (Critical callout)

title: Use Exact Same Import Pattern in Functional Tests
description: The agent must use the exact same import pattern found in existing tests when writing functional tests. Deviating from the recorded pattern is explicitly prohibited because doing so causes every test to fail with import or resolution errors.
acceptance_criteria: You must use the exact same pattern in your functional tests — getting this wrong means every test fails with import/resolution errors.
proposed_source_ref: Step 3: Read Existing Tests, paragraph 2 (Critical callout)

title: Consult the Six-Language Import Pattern Matrix
description: The agent must consult references/functional_tests.md § 'Import Pattern' for the full six-language matrix when determining or validating the import pattern to record and apply. This reference is the authoritative source for language-specific import conventions.
acceptance_criteria: See references/functional_tests.md § "Import Pattern" for the full six-language matrix.
proposed_source_ref: Step 3: Read Existing Tests, paragraph 2 (Critical callout)

title: Identify Integration Test Runners
description: As part of Step 3, the agent must identify integration test runners — scripts or test files that exercise the system end-to-end against real external services such as APIs and databases — and note their existence and patterns.
acceptance_criteria: Identify integration test runners. Look for scripts or test files that exercise the system end-to-end against real external services (APIs, databases, etc.).
proposed_source_ref: Step 3: Read Existing Tests, paragraph 3 (Identify integration test runners)

title: Record Integration Test Runner Patterns for RUN_INTEGRATION_TESTS.md
description: The agent must note the patterns of identified integration test runners. These patterns are required inputs for producing the RUN_INTEGRATION_TESTS.md artifact in a later step.
acceptance_criteria: Note their patterns — you'll need them for RUN_INTEGRATION_TESTS.md.
proposed_source_ref: Step 3: Read Existing Tests, paragraph 3 (Identify integration test runners)

title: Process each spec document section by section
description: When reading specifications, the orchestrator must walk each spec document section by section rather than skimming or selective reading. Every section must be visited in order to ensure no testable claims are missed.
acceptance_criteria: Walk each spec document section by section.
proposed_source_ref: Step 4: Read the Specifications, paragraph 1, sentence 1

title: Identify testable requirement for every spec section
description: For every section visited in a spec document, the orchestrator must explicitly ask and answer the question 'What testable requirement does this state?' No section may be passed over without this evaluation.
acceptance_criteria: For every section, ask: 'What testable requirement does this state?'
proposed_source_ref: Step 4: Read the Specifications, paragraph 1, sentence 2

title: Record spec requirements lacking corresponding tests as gaps
description: Spec requirements that have no corresponding functional tests must be recorded explicitly. These recorded gaps represent the set of tests that the functional test suite must close.
acceptance_criteria: Record spec requirements without corresponding tests — these are the gaps the functional tests must close.
proposed_source_ref: Step 4: Read the Specifications, paragraph 1, sentence 3

title: Tag inferred requirements with confidence tier using prescribed format
description: When requirements are inferred from tests, types, or code behavior rather than stated explicitly in specs, each such requirement must be tagged with its confidence tier using the format '[Req: tier — source]' as defined in Step 1.
acceptance_criteria: If using inferred requirements (from tests, types, or code behavior), tag each with its confidence tier using the `[Req: tier — source]` format defined in Step 1.
proposed_source_ref: Step 4: Read the Specifications, paragraph 2, sentence 1

title: Include inferred requirements in QUALITY.md scenarios
description: Inferred requirements must feed into the QUALITY.md scenarios section. They must not be silently discarded; they are a required input to the quality scenario generation step.
acceptance_criteria: Inferred requirements feed into QUALITY.md scenarios.
proposed_source_ref: Step 4: Read the Specifications, paragraph 2, sentence 2

title: Flag inferred requirements for user review in Phase 7
description: All inferred requirements must be explicitly flagged for user review during Phase 7. This flag ensures the user can validate requirements that were derived from code behavior rather than stated in formal specifications.
acceptance_criteria: Inferred requirements should be flagged for user review in Phase 7.
proposed_source_ref: Step 4: Read the Specifications, paragraph 2, sentence 2

title: Read Actual Function Signatures, Never Guess
description: Before writing any test, the agent must read the actual function definition for each function it plans to test to obtain parameter names, types, and defaults. It must not infer or guess the function signature from usage context or call sites — the function definition itself is the authoritative source.
acceptance_criteria: Don't guess from usage context — read the function definition and any documentation
proposed_source_ref: Step 4b, item 1, first sentence

title: Read Language-Specific Documentation for Signatures
description: When reading function signatures, the agent must also read the language-appropriate documentation attached to each function: Python docstrings, Java/Scala Javadoc or ScalaDoc, TypeScript type annotations, Go godoc comments, and Rust doc comments and type signatures. Skipping language-specific documentation when it is present is not acceptable.
acceptance_criteria: Python docstrings, Java/Scala Javadoc/ScalaDoc, TypeScript type annotations, Go godoc comments, Rust doc comments and type signatures
proposed_source_ref: Step 4b, item 1, parenthetical language list

title: Read Real Data Files from Known Project Directories
description: If the project contains items files, fixture files, config files, or sample data located in directories such as pipelines/, fixtures/, test_data/, or examples/, the agent must read those files before writing tests. The agent must not fabricate data shapes from memory or convention.
acceptance_criteria: If the project has items files, fixture files, config files, or sample data (in `pipelines/`, `fixtures/`, `test_data/`, `examples/`), read them
proposed_source_ref: Step 4b, item 2

title: Test Fixtures Must Match Real Data Shape Exactly
description: Any test fixture or synthetic test data the agent creates must conform exactly to the shape of real data files found in the project. Fields, keys, types, and structure must match; the agent must not approximate or simplify the real data shape in fixtures.
acceptance_criteria: Your test fixtures must match the real data shape exactly
proposed_source_ref: Step 4b, item 2, final sentence

title: Read Existing Test Fixtures Before Creating New Ones
description: Before creating any test fixtures, the agent must read the existing test files to determine how they construct test data. The agent must examine the actual fixture-creation code, not assume a pattern.
acceptance_criteria: Read existing test fixtures — How do existing tests create test data?
proposed_source_ref: Step 4b, item 3, first two sentences

title: Copy Exact Keys from Existing Test Fixture Patterns
description: When existing tests build configuration dictionaries or structured data with specific keys, the agent must use those exact keys in its own fixtures. Inventing or renaming keys relative to those used in existing test fixtures is not permitted.
acceptance_criteria: If they build config dicts with specific keys, use those exact keys
proposed_source_ref: Step 4b, item 3, final sentence

title: Check Dependency Manifest to Determine Available Libraries
description: The agent must check the project's dependency manifest file to determine which libraries and versions are actually installed before writing any tests. Applicable manifests include requirements.txt, build.sbt, package.json, pom.xml, build.gradle, go.mod, and Cargo.toml.
acceptance_criteria: Check the project's dependency manifest (`requirements.txt`, `build.sbt`, `package.json`, `pom.xml`/`build.gradle`, `go.mod`, `Cargo.toml`) to see what's actually available
proposed_source_ref: Step 4b, item 4, first two sentences

title: Must Not Write Tests Requiring Unavailable Library Features
description: The agent must not write tests that depend on library features or APIs that are not present in the installed version of that library, as determined by reading the dependency manifest. Writing tests for non-installed dependencies causes test-suite failures unrelated to the code under test.
acceptance_criteria: Don't write tests that depend on library features that aren't installed
proposed_source_ref: Step 4b, item 4, third sentence

title: Use Skip Mechanism When Dependency May Be Missing
description: If a dependency required by a test might not be installed in all environments, the agent must use the test framework's skip mechanism rather than letting the test fail with an import or runtime error. The specific skip API to use is determined per framework as documented in references/functional_tests.md § 'Library version awareness'.
acceptance_criteria: If a dependency might be missing, use the test framework's skip mechanism — see `references/functional_tests.md` § "Library version awareness" for framework-specific examples
proposed_source_ref: Step 4b, item 4, final two sentences

title: Record a Function Call Map for Every Function Under Test
description: The agent must produce and record a function call map before writing tests. The map must cover every function the agent plans to test, serving as a guard against calling functions with wrong arguments — identified as the most common test failure.
acceptance_criteria: Record a function call map: for each function you plan to test, write down its name, module, parameters, and what it returns
proposed_source_ref: Step 4b, final paragraph

title: Function Call Map Must Contain Name, Module, Parameters, and Return Value
description: Each entry in the function call map must record four fields: the function's name, the module it belongs to, its parameters, and what it returns. An entry that omits any of these four fields is incomplete and does not satisfy this requirement.
acceptance_criteria: for each function you plan to test, write down its name, module, parameters, and what it returns
proposed_source_ref: Step 4b, final paragraph, enumerated fields

title: Convert Each Defensive Pattern Into a Fitness-to-Purpose Scenario
description: Every defensive code pattern discovered during Step 5 must be converted into at least one fitness-to-purpose scenario. The section treats defensive patterns as direct evidence of past failures or known risks, and each such pattern must produce a corresponding fitness-to-purpose scenario that validates the system behaves correctly under the conditions the defensive code was written to handle.
acceptance_criteria: Each one becomes a fitness-to-purpose scenario and a boundary test
proposed_source_ref: Step 5: Find the Skeletons section, sentence beginning 'Each one becomes'

title: Convert Each Defensive Pattern Into a Boundary Test
description: Every defensive code pattern discovered during Step 5 must be converted into at least one boundary test. Distinct from the fitness-to-purpose scenario requirement, this requires a separate boundary test artifact per pattern — verifying edge and failure conditions at the boundary the defensive code was written to guard.
acceptance_criteria: Each one becomes a fitness-to-purpose scenario and a boundary test
proposed_source_ref: Step 5: Find the Skeletons section, sentence beginning 'Each one becomes'

title: Consult references/defensive_patterns.md for Search Approach and Grep Patterns
description: The reviewer must read references/defensive_patterns.md to obtain the systematic search approach, grep patterns, and conversion methodology for turning defensive pattern findings into fitness-to-purpose scenarios and boundary tests. This reference document is the normative guide for executing Step 5 and must not be skipped.
acceptance_criteria: Read references/defensive_patterns.md for the systematic search approach, grep patterns, and how to convert findings into fitness-to-purpose scenarios and boundary tests
proposed_source_ref: Step 5: Find the Skeletons section, line beginning 'Read references/defensive_patterns.md'

title: Find At Least 2-3 Defensive Patterns Per Core Source File
description: The minimum acceptable yield for Step 5 is 2 to 3 defensive patterns per core source file. Falling below this threshold indicates insufficient depth of analysis; fewer findings are a signal that the reviewer is skimming rather than fully reading the source.
acceptance_criteria: Minimum bar: at least 2–3 defensive patterns per core source file. If you find fewer, you're skimming
proposed_source_ref: Step 5: Find the Skeletons section, paragraph beginning 'Minimum bar'

title: Read Function Bodies Not Just Signatures When Searching for Defensive Patterns
description: During the defensive pattern search, the reviewer must read full function bodies rather than relying solely on function signatures. Reading only signatures is explicitly characterized as skimming and will cause the minimum pattern-per-file threshold to be missed.
acceptance_criteria: If you find fewer, you're skimming — read function bodies, not just signatures
proposed_source_ref: Step 5: Find the Skeletons section, final sentence of the minimum bar paragraph

title: Search Status/State Fields to Identify State Machines
description: When performing state machine tracing, the reviewer must search for status and state fields in models, enums, or constants using field names such as `status`, `state`, `phase`, or `mode` to systematically locate all state machines present in the project.
acceptance_criteria: Search for status/state fields in models, enums, or constants (e.g., `status`, `state`, `phase`, `mode`).
proposed_source_ref: Step 5a section, 'How to find state machines' paragraph, first search directive

title: Search Guards That Check Status to Identify State Machines
description: When performing state machine tracing, the reviewer must search for guards that check status fields before allowing actions — such as `if status == "running"` or `match self.state` patterns — to ensure all state-dependent control flow is captured.
acceptance_criteria: Search for guards that check status before allowing actions (e.g., `if status == "running"`, `match self.state`).
proposed_source_ref: Step 5a section, 'How to find state machines' paragraph, second search directive

title: Search State Transition Assignments to Identify State Machines
description: When performing state machine tracing, the reviewer must search for state transitions — specifically assignments to status fields — to fully map how states change at runtime.
acceptance_criteria: Search for state transitions (assignments to status fields).
proposed_source_ref: Step 5a section, 'How to find state machines' paragraph, third search directive

title: Enumerate All Possible States for Each State Machine
description: For each state machine identified, the reviewer must enumerate every possible state by reading the enum, the constants, or by grepping for every value the field is assigned, and must list all of them.
acceptance_criteria: Read the enum, the constants, or grep for every value the field is assigned. List them all.
proposed_source_ref: Step 5a section, numbered list item 1: 'Enumerate all possible states'

title: Check Every State Consumer Handles All States
description: For each consumer of state — including UI handlers, API endpoints, and control flow guards — the reviewer must verify that it handles every possible state, not just a subset.
acceptance_criteria: For each consumer of state (UI handlers, API endpoints, control flow guards), check: does it handle every possible state?
proposed_source_ref: Step 5a section, numbered list item 2: 'For each consumer of state'

title: Flag Switch/Match Without Meaningful Default as a Gap
description: A `switch` or `match` statement that does not include a meaningful default case is evidence of incomplete state handling and must be recorded as a gap finding.
acceptance_criteria: A `switch`/`match` without a meaningful default... is a gap.
proposed_source_ref: Step 5a section, numbered list item 2, gap examples

title: Flag If/Elif Chains Not Covering All States as a Gap
description: An `if`/`elif` chain on a state field that does not cover all possible states is evidence of incomplete state handling and must be recorded as a gap finding.
acceptance_criteria: An `if/elif` chain that doesn't cover all states, is a gap.
proposed_source_ref: Step 5a section, numbered list item 2, gap examples

title: Check Reachability of Every State via Transitions
description: For each state transition identified, the reviewer must verify that every possible state can actually be reached through some execution path — states that exist but are never transitioned into are a category of gap.
acceptance_criteria: For each state transition, check: can you reach every state?
proposed_source_ref: Step 5a section, numbered list item 3: 'For each state transition'

title: Check for States That Can Be Entered But Never Left
description: The reviewer must check whether any state can be entered but never exited — a state from which no transition leads to another state is a dead-end gap that traps the system.
acceptance_criteria: Are there states you can enter but never leave?
proposed_source_ref: Step 5a section, numbered list item 3: 'For each state transition'

title: Check for States That Block Operations That Should Be Available
description: The reviewer must check whether any states block operations that should logically be available to the user or system in that state, identifying cases where a needed action is prevented by the current state value.
acceptance_criteria: Are there states that block operations that should be available?
proposed_source_ref: Step 5a section, numbered list item 3: 'For each state transition'

title: Record State Machine Gaps as Findings
description: All state machine gaps discovered during the trace — including unhandled states, unreachable states, dead-end states, and states that block needed operations — must be recorded as findings.
acceptance_criteria: Record gaps as findings.
proposed_source_ref: Step 5a section, numbered list item 4: 'Record gaps as findings'

title: Record Status Guard That Excludes a Needed State as a Real Bug
description: When a status guard allows a user-needed action for one state (e.g., 'running') but not for another state the user may be in (e.g., 'stuck'), this must be recorded as a real bug, not merely a gap or note.
acceptance_criteria: A status guard that allows action X for 'running' but not for 'stuck' is a real bug if the user needs to perform action X on stuck processes.
proposed_source_ref: Step 5a section, numbered list item 4, first example finding

title: Record Terminal State Without Cleanup Trigger as a Real Bug
description: When a process can enter a terminal state but that state never triggers the expected cleanup logic, this must be recorded as a real bug, since cleanup will silently not occur.
acceptance_criteria: A process that enters a terminal state but never triggers cleanup is a real bug.
proposed_source_ref: Step 5a section, numbered list item 4, second example finding

title: Read Schema Definitions When Validation Layer Exists
description: When the project under review contains a validation layer — including Pydantic models in Python, JSON Schema definitions, TypeScript interfaces or Zod schemas, Java Bean Validation annotations, or Scala case class codecs — the skill must read those schema definitions during Step 5b before proceeding.
acceptance_criteria: If the project has a validation layer (Pydantic models in Python, JSON Schema, TypeScript interfaces/Zod schemas, Java Bean Validation annotations, Scala case class codecs), read the schema definitions now.
proposed_source_ref: Step 5b section, paragraph 1, first sentence

title: Record Schema Accept/Reject Behavior for Each Defensively-Patterned Field
description: For every field for which a defensive pattern was identified in Step 5, the skill must record what the schema accepts versus what it rejects. This creates a per-field mapping that links defensive code findings to schema constraints and enables the generation of valid boundary tests in later passes.
acceptance_criteria: For every field you found a defensive pattern for, record what the schema accepts vs. rejects.
proposed_source_ref: Step 5b section, paragraph 1, second sentence

title: Consult references/schema_mapping.md for Mapping Format
description: The skill must read the reference document at references/schema_mapping.md to obtain the correct mapping format for schema accept/reject recordings and to understand why schema mapping matters for writing valid boundary tests.
acceptance_criteria: Read `references/schema_mapping.md` for the mapping format and why this matters for writing valid boundary tests.
proposed_source_ref: Step 5b section, paragraph 2 (bold directive)

title: Complete Step 6 Before Structured Pattern Selection
description: Step 6 (Domain-Knowledge Risk Analysis) is the primary bug-hunting pass for library and framework codebases and must be completed before selecting any structured patterns. The orchestrator must enforce this ordering so that domain-risk findings can inform pattern selection.
acceptance_criteria: Complete it before selecting any structured patterns.
proposed_source_ref: Step 6 opening paragraph, sentence 2

title: Write Quality Risks to EXPLORATION.md Immediately
description: Results of the domain-knowledge risk analysis must be written to the `## Quality Risks` section of EXPLORATION.md immediately upon generation. The orchestrator must not hold them in memory or defer writing until later in the workflow.
acceptance_criteria: Write the results to the `## Quality Risks` section of EXPLORATION.md immediately — do not hold them in memory.
proposed_source_ref: Step 6 opening paragraph, sentence 3

title: Use Both Code Exploration and Domain Knowledge as Sources
description: Step 6 must draw on exactly two sources: direct code exploration of the project under review, and training-based domain knowledge about what goes wrong in systems of that type. Analysis that uses only one source (e.g., only code reading without domain reasoning, or only generic domain knowledge without code inspection) does not satisfy this step.
acceptance_criteria: This step uses two sources — not just code exploration, but your training knowledge of what goes wrong in similar systems.
proposed_source_ref: Step 6, paragraph 2

title: Generate At Least Five Ranked Failure Scenarios
description: The output of Step 6 must include a minimum of five failure scenarios drawn from domain knowledge, even when the analyst has not directly observed these failures in the codebase. The scenarios must be ranked (e.g., by priority or severity). Fewer than five scenarios or an unranked list fails the gate.
acceptance_criteria: Generate at least 5 ranked failure scenarios from this knowledge.
proposed_source_ref: Step 6, paragraph after domain-knowledge questions

title: Bug Hypotheses Must Include File-Path and Line-Number Citations
description: Every failure scenario produced in Step 6 must be expressed as a specific bug hypothesis that includes a file path and line number citation into the actual codebase. Abstract or module-level citations (e.g., 'somewhere in auth.py') are insufficient; the citation must be specific enough to direct a reviewer to an exact location.
acceptance_criteria: Write them as specific bug hypotheses with file-path and line-number citations, ranked by priority.
proposed_source_ref: Step 6, paragraph after domain-knowledge questions

title: Bug Hypotheses Must Be Ranked by Priority
description: The failure scenarios written in Step 6 must be ordered by priority so that the highest-risk hypotheses appear first. An unordered list of hypotheses, even if otherwise well-formed, does not satisfy the ranking requirement.
acceptance_criteria: Write them as specific bug hypotheses with file-path and line-number citations, ranked by priority.
proposed_source_ref: Step 6, paragraph after domain-knowledge questions

title: Each Scenario Must Follow the Prescribed Framing Format
description: Each failure scenario must be framed using the required template: 'Because [code at file:line] does [X], a [domain-specific edge case] will produce [wrong behavior] instead of [correct behavior].' This format ensures each hypothesis is anchored to a specific code site, names a concrete triggering input, and articulates both the actual and expected output. Scenarios that omit any element of this structure are malformed.
acceptance_criteria: Frame each as: 'Because [code at file:line] does [X], a [domain-specific edge case] will produce [wrong behavior] instead of [correct behavior].'
proposed_source_ref: Step 6, paragraph after domain-knowledge questions

title: Scenarios Must Be Grounded in Actually Explored Code
description: After framing each domain-knowledge hypothesis, the analyst must ground it in the actual code that was explored during the pass, for example by citing a specific function and line range with a note about what to verify. Hypotheses that are purely theoretical and not connected to any specific code artifact in the project under review do not satisfy this requirement.
acceptance_criteria: Then ground them in the actual code you explored: 'Read persistence.py line ~340 (save_state): verify temp file + rename pattern.'
proposed_source_ref: Step 6, paragraph after domain-knowledge questions

title: Quality Risks Must Not List Defensive Patterns the Code Already Has
description: The `## Quality Risks` section must not enumerate defensive patterns that are already present in the codebase (i.e., things the code does right). Listing existing safeguards as risks is a reassurance exercise and will fail the quality gate. Only gaps, weaknesses, and hypothesized failure modes belong in the section.
acceptance_criteria: A Quality Risks section that lists defensive patterns the code already has (things the code does right) is not a risk analysis — it is a reassurance exercise and will not find bugs.
proposed_source_ref: Step 6, Anti-patterns that fail the gate, item 1

title: Quality Risks Must Not List Risky Modules Without Specific Failure Scenarios
description: Naming modules or components as risky without supplying a specific failure scenario for each is not actionable and fails the gate. Every entry in `## Quality Risks` must describe a concrete failure scenario, not merely identify a location or category of concern.
acceptance_criteria: A section that lists risky modules without specific failure scenarios is not actionable.
proposed_source_ref: Step 6, Anti-patterns that fail the gate, item 2

title: Quality Risks Must Not Conclude Mature Libraries Are Unlikely to Have Bugs
description: The `## Quality Risks` section must not contain a conclusion that a mature or well-tested library is unlikely to have basic bugs.
